author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Donatist Controversy.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

originalBook: In which Augustin replies to all the several statements in the letter of Petilianus, as though disputing with an adversary face to face.
title: Chapter 84

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Chapter 84.—183.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "But if authority had been given by some law for persons to be compelled to what is good, you yourselves, unhappy men, ought to have been compelled by us to embrace the purest faith.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But far be it, far be it from our conscience to compel any one to embrace our faith."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: No one is indeed to be compelled to embrace the faith against his will; but by the severity, or one might rather say, by the mercy of God, it is common for treachery to be chastised with the scourge of tribulation.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it the case, because the best morals are chosen by freedom of will, that therefore the worst morals are not punished by integrity of law?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But yet discipline to punish an evil manner of living is out of the question, except where principles of good living which had been learned have come to be despised.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: If any laws, therefore, have been enacted against you, you are not thereby forced to do well, but are only prevented from doing ill.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For no one can do well unless he has deliberately chosen, and unless he has loved what is in free will; but the fear of punishment, even if it does not share in the pleasures of a good conscience, at any rate keeps the evil desire from escaping beyond the bounds of thought.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Who are they, however, that have enacted adverse laws by which your audacity could be repressed?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Are they not those of whom the apostle says that "they bear not the sword in vain; for they are the ministers of God, revengers to execute wrath upon them that do evil?"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The whole question therefore is, whether you are not doing ill, who are charged by the whole world with the sacrilege of so great a schism.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And yet, neglecting the discussion of this question, you talk on irrelevant matters; and while you live as robbers, you boast that you die as martyrs.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And, through fear either of the laws themselves, or of the odium which you might incur, or else because you are unequal to the task of resisting, I do not say so many men, but so many Catholic nations, you even glory in your gentleness, that you do not compel any to join your party.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: According to your way of talking, the hawk, when he has been prevented by flight from carrying off the fowls, might call himself a dove.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For when have you ever had the power without using it?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And hence you show how you would do more if you only could.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: When Julian, envying the peace of Christ, restored to you the churches which belonged to unity, who could tell of all the massacres which were committed by you, when the very devils rejoiced with you at the opening of their temples?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In the war with Firmus and his party, let Mauritania Cæsariensis itself be asked to tell us what the Moor Rogatus suffered at your hands.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: In the time of Gildo, because one of your colleagues was his intimate friend, let the followers of Maximianus be our witnesses to their sufferings.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if one might appeal to Felicianus himself, who is now with you, on his oath, whether Optatus did not compel him against his will to return to your communion, he would not dare to open his lips, especially if the people of Musti could behold his face, who were witnesses to everything that was done.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But let them, as I have said, be witnesses to what they have suffered at the hands of those with whom they acted in such wise towards Rogatus.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The Catholic Church herself, though strengthened by the assistance of Catholic princes ruling by land and sea, was savagely attacked by hostile troops in arms under Optatus.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: It was this that first made it necessary to urge before the vicar Seranus that the law should be put in force against you which imposes a fine of ten pounds of gold, which none of you have ever paid to this very day, and yet you charge us with cruelty.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But where could you find a milder course of proceeding, than that crimes of such magnitude on your part should be punished by the imposition of a pecuniary fine?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Or who could enumerate all the deeds which you commit in the places which you hold, of your own sovereign will and pleasure, each one as he can, without any friendship on the part of judges or any others in authority?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Who is there of our party, among the inhabitants of our towns, who has not either learned something of this sort from those who came before him, or experienced it for himself?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is it not the case that at Hippo, where I am, there are not wanting some who remember that your leader Faustinus gave orders, in the time of his supreme power, in consequence of the scanty numbers of the Catholics in the place, that no one should bake their bread for them, insomuch that a baker, who was the tenant of one of our deacons, threw away the bread of his landlord unbaked, and though he was not sentenced to exile under any law, he cut him off from all share in the necessaries of life not only in a Roman state, but even in his own country, and not only in his own country, but in his own house?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why, even lately, as I myself recall with mourning to this day, did not Crispinus of Calama, one of your party, having bought a property, and that only copy-hold, boldly and unhesitatingly immerse in the waters of a second baptism no less than eighty souls, murmuring with miserable groans under the sole influence of terror; and this in a farm belonging to the Catholic emperors, by whose laws you were forbidden even to be in any Roman city?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But what else was it, save such deeds as these of yours, that made it necessary for the very laws to be passed of which you complain?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: The laws, indeed, are very far from being proportionate to your offenses; but, such as they are, you may thank yourselves for their existence.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Indeed, should we not certainly be driven on all sides from the country by the furious attacks of your Circumcelliones, who fight under your command in furious troops, unless we held you as hostages in the towns, who might well be unwilling to endure under any circumstances the mere gaze of the people, and the censure of all honorable men. from very shame, if not from fear?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Do not therefore say, "Far be it, far be it from our conscience, to force any one to embrace our faith."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For you do it when you can; and when you do not do it, it is because you are unable, either from fear of the laws or the odium which would accompany it, or because of the numbers of those who would resist.
